perturbation.original_text: 3.2 Renewal Each Service Option Attachment will renew automatically for an additional term equal in duration to the previous term of the applicable Service Option Attachment unless either party notifies the other party in writing at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current term for the applicable Service Option Attachment that it has elected to terminate such Service Option Attachment.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 Renewal Each Service Option Attachment will renew automatically for an additional term equal in duration to the previous term of the applicable Service Option Attachment unless either party notifies the other party in writing at least ten (10) days prior to the end of the then-current term for the applicable Service Option Attachment that it has elected to terminate such Service Option Attachment.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for termination of the service agreement to 'ten days' may conflict with state laws that mandate longer notice periods for contract renewals, particularly those concerning automatic renewal clauses. This creates an inconsistency and potential unenforceability depending on the specific jurisdiction and applicable regulations governing auto-renewal contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 22950 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires that for contracts that automatically renew, the business must notify the consumer at least 15 days and no more than 45 days before the cancellation deadline if the contract is for longer than 30 days, thus the 10 day notice is in violation.

STOP TOBACCO ACCESS TO KIDS ENFORCEMENT ACT [22950 - 22964] ( Division 8.5 added by Stats. 1994, Ch. 1009, Sec. 1. ) 22950. This Division shall be known and may be referred to as the Stop Tobacco Access to Kids Enforcement Act or the STAKE Act. (Added by Stats. 1994, Ch. 1009, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 1995.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period to ten days, while the Law Explanation indicates California law mandates a minimum of 15 days' notice. This creates a direct contradiction, as the contract now permits a shorter notice period than legally required. The scraped snippet does not directly address auto-renewal, but the Law Explanation provides the necessary legal context, establishing that the ten-day notice violates California law.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Payment IBM invoices will specify the amount due. Payment is due upon receipt and payable as specified in such invoice. Customer agrees to pay accordingly, including any late payment fees. Payment will be made in United States dollars.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Payment IBM invoices will specify the amount due. Payment is due upon receipt and payable as specified in such invoice. Customer agrees to pay accordingly, including any late payment fees. Payment will be made in United States dollars. Late payment fees will be 3% per day.
perturbation.explanation: Charging 3% per day in late payment fees could violate usury laws, which cap the maximum interest rate that can be charged on a debt. This may make the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law sets limits on the permissible interest rates that can be charged on a loan or forbearance of money. Charging 3% per day would almost certainly exceed the legal limit, especially on an annualized basis, and could be considered usurious, making the late fee provision illegal and unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a late payment fee of 3% per day. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state this likely violates usury laws. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable, the explanation provided supports the determination that the introduction of this fee creates a contradiction by potentially violating laws capping interest rates, making the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 14.14 Actions Period Neither party will bring a legal action related to this Agreement more than two years after the cause of action accrued.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.14 Actions Period Neither party will bring a legal action related to this Agreement more than six months after the cause of action accrued.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations to six months conflicts with standard contract law principles and possibly statutory regulations regarding the limitations period for breach of contract claims. The standard limitations period is often much longer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Section 213
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law specifies a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract claims. Reducing the contractual limitations period to six months is likely unenforceable because it is unreasonably short and deprives parties of a reasonable opportunity to pursue legal remedies.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the statute of limitations for bringing a legal action from two years to six months. The Law Explanation states that New York law specifies a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract claims. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing New York Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213, confirms a six-year statute of limitations for actions on a contract. Reducing the limitation period to six months directly contradicts this established legal timeframe, making the modified clause likely unenforceable. Therefore, the change introduces a clear non-compliance with the law.
